reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher professionalism depends on (a) workload transparency and (b) clear, stable assessment criteria that do not impose hidden cognitive load; teacher autonomy requires that admission-rule changes do not cascade into unmanaged classroom composition volatility or unvalidated assessment design imposed on schools.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers' professional autonomy, workload, and assessment stability — we speak for those who will administer the new assessment and manage the resulting classroom compositions.
reactions.public_good_frame: Sustainable, professionally credible admission reform that does not shift hidden equity costs onto teachers or create assessment burden without proper validation and preparation time.
reactions.argument: S1's core mechanism—replacing the central written exam with a 'less preparation-sensitive' aptitude or portfolio assessment—transfers design and administration risk onto schools and teachers without specifying who bears the validation cost, timeline, or liability if the new instrument proves coachable-by-other-means or fails predictive validity. The scenario acknowledges (correctly) that 'no validated Hungarian instrument currently exists' and proposes a 'multi-year psychometric development' process. Teachers will be asked to administer, interpret, and defend an assessment whose properties are still being validated. This is a workload and autonomy issue: we are being asked to implement a tool whose reliability we have no stake in designing and whose failure is then attributed to us. Additionally, SES-weighted quotas introduce classroom composition uncertainty—if reweighting is implemented without parallel investment in differentiated-instruction training (not mentioned in S1), teachers face increased heterogeneity without preparation. The scenario's 'condition' that 'a workable SES indicator or proxy can be constructed' is not a safe assumption for workload: any SES proxy will require verification at application time, which will either fall on schools (workload) or create application friction that schools must manage (autonomy erosion). The cross-maintainer accountability mandate is neutral to us if uniformly enforced, but only if enforcement does not create compliance audit burden on state schools while non-state maintainers are harder to monitor (a risk the scenario flags as 'weak' evidence but does not resolve).
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S1 if: (1) Oktatási Hivatal validates the new assessment with pilot groups of real students and teachers, publishes full reliability/validity evidence, and delivers ready-to-use assessment tools and scoring rubrics before national rollout; (2) differentiated-instruction training is a mandatory, paid, timetabled component of the rollout, not a recommendation; (3) SES weighting uses only a data-minimized proxy (e.g., school-level disadvantage indicator, not family-level income queries administered by schools) to avoid shifting verification burden to teachers; (4) a binding cross-maintainer accountability rule is legislated and monitored uniformly, not left to voluntary compliance; (5) workload impact is independently audited before and after implementation, with contractual commitments to reduce teaching time or caseload if administration of the new assessment exceeds specified thresholds.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher employment security and fair deployment depend on predictable, long-term workforce planning; gradual phase-down of selective track places creates a decade-long regime of parallel rules, unequal treatment of cohorts, and deferred redeployment uncertainty that erodes both security and morale.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers currently employed in selective-track positions and those training to enter the profession; we defend the interest in clarity about employment continuity and professional identity.
reactions.public_good_frame: Workforce stability and teacher morale are prerequisites for educational quality during any reform; a decade-long phase-down creates unnecessary uncertainty and may drive exodus of experienced selective-track teachers at the moment when the system most needs their expertise to manage the transition.
reactions.argument: S2 proposes a 'multi-year phase-down' (explicitly 'year 8-10' in the implementation section), during which state-maintained selective intake is capped while non-state maintainers are assumed not to expand. Teachers in the selective track face a decade of uncertainty: their positions are not eliminated immediately (which would trigger clear redundancy procedures and severance), but are capped and gradually compressed as new cohorts enter. The scenario acknowledges 'parallel-regime administration' and 'grandfathering of enrolled cohorts,' which means a 10-year window of teaching under two sets of rules simultaneously—some classes are the old, full cohort; others are capped. This creates: (1) deployment unpredictability—redeployment to general schools is deferred, so teachers do not know when/if they will move; (2) professional identity erosion—selective-track teachers are not recognized as serving a legitimate track anymore, but are not yet redeployed; (3) morale damage—the scenario explicitly flags that 'non-state maintainers can absorb selective tracks and bypass national consolidation plans,' which means state-sector teachers see colleagues move to church/foundation schools while they wait. The scenario also notes 'teacher redeployment from shrinking selective-track positions to general-school posts — cost: medium, given national wage-grid rigidity,' which means wages do not compensate for change, and movement to general schools is likely seen as demotion. The cost_checker has flagged the original 'medium, transitional' cost estimate as 'likely underestimating duration-driven costs'—meaning redeployment support (retraining, relocation allowances, differentiated-instruction training) is chronically underfunded in this scenario. No teacher input into the timeline, redeployment criteria, or retraining quality is mentioned. This violates basic employment-security norms and is likely to trigger union resistance (documented by political_feasibility's Round 06 analysis). The assumption that 'demographic contraction provides political cover' is a policy-maker assumption, not a labor-market one: teachers experience contraction as closure risk, not as political cover.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would conditionally support S2 if: (1) phase-down is accelerated to a maximum of 3-4 years (not 8-10), so that deployment uncertainty is brief and retraining/redeployment can be front-loaded; (2) teachers in selective tracks are guaranteed redeployment to general schools at equivalent salary and status, with a dedicated retraining budget (not 'medium cost' but clearly specified, per-teacher allowance); (3) retraining in differentiated instruction is mandatory, salaried, and scheduled during contracted planning time, not summers; (4) a binding agreement with non-state maintainers is negotiated (legislated if necessary) to prevent absorbing displaced selective-track demand, so that redeployment targets are predictable; (5) annual consultation with teacher unions on the implementation schedule, with power to adjust timeline if retraining or redeployment is not meeting agreed quality standards. Absent these conditions, S2 is a way of deferring the hard choices of structural reform onto teachers' employment security.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher professionalism and workload are sustainable only with adequate class size, support staff, and preparation time for heterogeneous instruction; S3's bundled assumption of 'upgraded teacher training' is not resourced or scheduled concretely, and the scenario's own evidence acknowledges that 'teacher supply and quality in comprehensive schools can be upgraded fast enough' is an unmet assumption.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers across all school types; we defend the interest in manageable class sizes, adequate planning time, and realistic training timelines that do not frontload implementation while backloading investment.
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural reform to comprehensive systems can improve equity, but only if accompanied by real investment in teacher capacity and working conditions; asking teachers to teach larger, more heterogeneous classes while training is promised 'in years 1-4' is asking teachers to absorb the risk of reform failure.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that proposes a genuinely structural change—abolishing selective entry at age 10/12 and moving to a comprehensive lower-secondary system until age 14. The international evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Pekkarinen, Uusitalo & Kerr 2009) shows that early tracking increases SES-outcome gradients, and that delaying tracking can reduce them. However, the Finnish precedent that S3 cites explicitly bundled tracking-delay with massive teacher investment: smaller class sizes, more planning time, specialist diagnostic teachers, and an entire reframing of the teacher role toward differentiation. S3 acknowledges this: 'Bundling the reform with upgraded teacher training and early diagnostic/remedial support is necessary for equity gains to materialize.' But the implementation timeline proposed is: 'Year 1-4: Ministry of Education commissions and funds a national teacher retraining programme.' This means: years 1-4, teachers are already teaching comprehensive classes (mixed-ability, larger than before because selective tracks are gone) with no new training yet completed. The 'early diagnostic and remedial support system build-out' is also 'year 1-3 pilot, year 4-6 national rollout'—again, schools are managing increased heterogeneity for years 1-3 without the support system. The cost_checker has flagged the teacher retraining cost as 'high, though no precise per-cohort/per-district unit cost is available,' which means the actual cost of retraining is unknown. The scenario also notes that 'cost_checker notes this absence itself understates likely total exposure given the scale of the workforce affected'—meaning the actual cost is likely higher than estimated, which means funding is at risk of being cut. Implementation_planning has documented that 'Hungary's teacher shortage is binding'—meaning there are not enough teachers to cover current classes. S3 removes selective tracks, which reduces teaching positions in those tracks but increases heterogeneity in remaining positions. Net effect: fewer jobs but harder work without additional support staff or training. The political_risks section notes 'Teacher union non-cooperation if retraining is not resourced with dedicated planning time rather than lump-sum funding'—and the scenario does not specify how planning time is to be allocated or funded. The scenario also acknowledges that 'whether teacher supply/quality upgrades can occur fast enough to avoid the Finnish-reform's warned-of heterogeneity/flight dynamics' is an unresolved uncertainty with 'low confidence.' In other words: S3 admits that this is a plausible failure mode, and we are being asked to implement it anyway without confidence it will work. This is asking teachers to absorb the risk of reform failure. If comprehensive schools fail because teachers are not trained and class sizes are too large, the blame will be on 'bad teaching,' not on inadequate resourcing. The most concerning element: S3 bundles the abolition of selective entry with teacher retraining, but retraining depends on budget approval ('Ministry of Finance: secure multi-year budgetary commitment'). If that commitment fails or is cut in fiscal consolidation, teachers are left with comprehensive classes and no training. This is a workload and autonomy issue because it makes teacher working conditions dependent on a political budget process, not a professional commitment.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S3 only if: (1) retraining in differentiated instruction begins one full year before the first comprehensive cohort enters, is mandatory, salaried, and includes at least 100 hours per teacher over that year; (2) maximum class sizes in comprehensive lower-secondary are legislated at 20-22 pupils (vs. current 28-30 in general schools), with staffing norms to match; (3) dedicated diagnostic and support staff (psychologists, remedial teachers, learning assistants) are added to the system before comprehensive entry begins, not piloted afterward; (4) a binding multi-year budget commitment (four-year minimum, cross-party agreement required to alter) is legislated and held in a protected budget line that cannot be cut in fiscal consolidation; (5) if retraining completion or class-size norms are not met by year 1 of comprehensive entry, comprehensive rollout is paused until the conditions are met, with no obligation on teachers to implement an under-resourced reform; (6) annual union-led review of teacher workload and conditions during the transition, with binding adjustment mechanisms if thresholds are exceeded. Without these, S3 is asking teachers to be the shock absorber for an under-funded structural reform.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher professionalism requires stable working conditions and predictable resource commitments; S4's promised compensation to general schools via TEIP-style funding and grade-retention reduction is workload-positive if durable, but the scenario itself flags budget vulnerability as 'a plausible first-cut target in fiscal consolidation,' which creates permanent uncertainty about whether the promised resources will materialize.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers in general schools and those in disadvantaged schools; we defend the interest in durable, adequately-resourced support for teaching in high-needs environments.
reactions.public_good_frame: Non-structural reforms can improve equity and working conditions if they come with sustained funding and clear, professional training; compensation that is politically fragile is worse than no compensation because it creates false promises and then withdraws support.
reactions.argument: S4 is the only scenario that does not attempt to change the selective-track structure itself; instead, it invests in 'targeted funding for disadvantaged schools' (TEIP-style) and 'rigorous national curriculum standards' while reducing grade retention. For teachers in general schools (the 85%+ of pupils not in selective tracks), this could be genuinely beneficial: more resources, clearer curriculum, and support for struggling pupils. The mechanism is sound: Portugal raised PISA scores 2006-2015 with TEIP-style packages. However, S4 has a critical workload vulnerability. The scenario itself acknowledges: 'targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is a plausible first-cut target in fiscal consolidation, undermining the durability of the compensation mechanism.' This is not a small caveat. Hungarian budgetary history includes multiple cycles of education-funding cuts during fiscal consolidation (e.g., 2010-2012). Teachers in disadvantaged schools would experience a boom-and-bust cycle: years 1-3, new resources arrive, class sizes drop, support staff are hired, morale rises. Years 4-7 (fiscal consolidation), cuts return, positions disappear, workload rises again. This creates worse morale than a stable, lower baseline because the expectation of durability is raised and then violated. Additionally, the scenario does not resource the 'national curriculum standards' or 'grade-retention-reduction support programmes' with dedicated teaching time. If curriculum standards are to be implemented, teachers need planning time. If grade-retention reduction requires diagnostic assessment and remedial instruction, teachers need support staff and time. The scenario lists these as 'cost: medium, recurring,' but does not specify whether this is new hiring, redeployment of existing staff, or unfunded mandates. Implementation_planning has flagged that 'Hungary's teacher shortage is binding,' which means 'medium, recurring cost' is likely to be absorbed by existing, overburdened staff. The political_risks section notes: 'Teacher union non-cooperation if retraining is not resourced with dedicated planning time rather than lump-sum funding'—and S4 does not address this explicitly. The scenario also notes that 'whether sustained, multi-year funding for targeted programmes can survive Hungarian budgetary and political cycles without reversal' has 'medium confidence' of success. This is honest, but it means we are being asked to implement a reform that the designers themselves only half-expect to survive. Finally, S4's equity impact is described as 'bounded' because it 'leaves the sorting mechanism itself intact and only compensates downstream.' For teachers, this means: selective-track teachers continue to teach selected cohorts; general-school teachers continue to teach the 'residual' cohort. The scenario does not address whether selective-track teachers' working conditions improve or worsen, only that they are untouched. This asymmetry matters for morale and for systemic coherence. Teachers in general schools will know they are being compensated for teaching a system that leaves them with the 'residual' cohort; selective-track teachers will know they are protected. This is not a sustainable professional settlement.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S4 if: (1) targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is legislated as a constitutional or special-budget commitment (not subject to discretionary fiscal consolidation), with a minimum 15-year durability clause; (2) curriculum standards are accompanied by a dedicated, annual planning-time allocation (e.g., 5 additional planning days per year, salaried) for teachers to implement them; (3) grade-retention reduction is supported by new diagnostic staff (educational psychologists, remedial teachers) hired specifically for this purpose, not absorbed by existing teachers; (4) annual, independent audit of teacher workload in disadvantaged schools, with triggers to restore funding or pause curriculum changes if workload exceeds professional standards; (5) selective-track teachers are also included in a workload/working-condition review, to ensure compensation is not seen as favoring disadvantaged schools while selective schools are implicitly deprioritized (which could trigger middle-class backlash and reversal). With these conditions, S4 is a viable, professionally-defensible path that invests in real capacity rather than promising structural change the system may not deliver. Without them, S4 is a bet-the-farm on political durability, which the scenario itself does not confidently predict.
